past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 8 years ago, well-controlled on medication
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 5 years ago, managed with metformin, HbA1c 6.8%
Chronic migraine without aura, well-controlled for past 3 years
Mild bilateral cataracts, diagnosed 2 years ago, not yet requiring surgery
family_history: Father died at age 82 of pneumonia, had no known cognitive issues
Mother alive at age 88, lives independently, no dementia
No family history of dementia or neurodegenerative disease

social_history.occupation: Architect at a mid-size firm, still actively working but has reduced her project load over the past year
social_history.education: Master's degree in architecture from UC Berkeley
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her husband in a two-story home; two adult children live out of state
social_history.alcohol: Rare, 1-2 glasses of wine per month
social_history.exercise: Yoga twice weekly, used to do daily walks but reports bumping into things more frequently so has reduced outdoor walking

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, date, and situation. MMSE 26/30 — lost 1 point on recall (2/3 at 5 minutes, retrieved third word with categorical cue), lost 2 points on intersecting pentagons (unable to accurately copy the figure despite multiple attempts), lost 1 point on written sentence (wrote a grammatically correct sentence but placed words overlapping on the line). Clock drawing mildly abnormal — numbers clustered on the right side of the clock face with spatial distortion, hands placed correctly. MoCA 23/30 with deficits primarily in visuospatial/executive tasks (cube copy failed, trail-making errors) and attention (serial 7s: 2 errors). Naming intact. Repetition intact. Comprehension intact. Verbal fluency adequate (16 animals in 1 minute). Registration 3/3. Calculation intact for simple operations.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Visual fields full to confrontation (formal perimetry not performed). Optic discs normal on fundoscopy. Face symmetric. Hearing intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone throughout. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No pronator drift. No tremor. No myoclonus.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact in all extremities. Vibration sense intact at great toes bilaterally. Proprioception intact at toes. Temperature sensation intact. Cortical sensory testing: difficulty with graphesthesia in both hands (could not identify numbers written on palm), impaired stereognosis bilaterally (misidentified a key as a coin, misidentified a paperclip as a pin). Simultaneous bilateral stimulation reveals left-sided visual extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing normal. Rapid alternating movements intact. However, patient has difficulty with bimanual coordination tasks and exhibits ideomotor apraxia when asked to pantomime using a comb or brushing teeth — performs the action on the object but cannot pantomime without the object present.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal base and stride length. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative. However, patient hesitates at doorways and misjudges distances when reaching for objects on the exam table.
neurological_exam.additional: No paratonic rigidity. No grasp reflex. No alien limb phenomenon. Balint syndrome screening: mild simultanagnosia (has difficulty describing a complex scene, tends to identify individual elements but misses the overall picture), optic ataxia (misreaches for objects placed in peripheral visual fields), ocular apraxia not clearly demonstrated. Gerstmann syndrome elements: finger agnosia present bilaterally, right-left confusion present, agraphia not observed, acalculia not observed.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive difficulty reading, navigating familiar spaces, and judging distances over the past 18 months
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Keiko Yamamoto is a 65-year-old right-handed architect who presents to the neurology outpatient clinic at the urging of her husband and a colleague for evaluation of progressive visuospatial difficulties over the past 18 months. She initially noticed that she was having increasing trouble reading architectural blueprints — she would lose her place on the page, misread dimensions, and have difficulty following the spatial layout of floor plans she would previously have interpreted effortlessly. She attributed this to her cataracts and saw her ophthalmologist, who noted only mild bilateral cataracts insufficient to explain her symptoms. Her corrected visual acuity was measured at 20/25 bilaterally. Over the following months, her symptoms progressed. She began having difficulty parking her car, frequently misjudging the distance to adjacent vehicles, and twice scraped her side mirror against the garage door frame, something that had never happened before. She reports that navigating through crowded spaces such as shopping malls or airports has become anxiety-provoking; she feels overwhelmed and cannot process the visual scene efficiently, describing it as 'everything comes at me at once but I can't make sense of it all together.' Her husband notes that she has begun reaching past objects when trying to pick them up, occasionally knocking over glasses at the dinner table. She has difficulty dressing, particularly buttoning her blouse and fastening her bra, and has switched to pullover tops and sports bras to avoid these tasks. She sometimes confuses left and right when giving or following directions. At work, a trusted junior colleague privately approached her husband to express concern that Ms. Yamamoto had made several unusual spatial errors on recent designs that required correction, including misaligning structural elements that she would ordinarily render perfectly. Reading for pleasure has become frustrating; she loses her place frequently, skips lines, and finds it exhausting to read more than a few pages. Notably, her memory for conversations, appointments, and recent events remains quite good. She remembers the details of meetings, recalls names and faces without difficulty, and has not shown the forgetfulness or repetitiveness that one might expect in typical Alzheimer disease. Her personality is unchanged — she remains warm, socially engaged, and insightful. She is aware that something is wrong and expresses significant anxiety and frustration about her declining abilities, worrying that she is developing early dementia. She denies headaches, seizures, hallucinations, tremor, weakness, numbness, or tingling. Sleep is unremarkable. Her mood has been anxious but not depressed.

findings.location: Bilateral parieto-occipital cortex, more prominent on the right

signal_characteristics.T1: Widened sulci in bilateral parietal and occipital lobes with associated cortical thinning, right greater than left
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No signal abnormality within the atrophic cortex
Confluent periventricular hyperintensities and scattered deep white matter punctate lesions, bilateral, symmetric

signal_characteristics.Coronal_T1: Hippocampal volumes are within normal limits for age, Scheltens MTA score 0-1 bilaterally

findings.location: Left caudate head, right corona radiata

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Small hyperintense foci with surrounding gliosis

volumetrics.hippocampal_volume: Bilateral hippocampal volumes within normal limits for age, Scheltens MTA score 0-1
volumetrics.ventricular_system: Mild lateral ventriculomegaly, possibly related to a combination of periventricular white matter disease and subtle cortical volume loss
volumetrics.cortical_thickness: Reduced cortical thickness in bilateral parietal and occipital regions, right greater than left; temporal and frontal cortical thickness relatively preserved

additional_observations: No evidence of acute hemorrhage on SWI
Two old microbleeds in the right parietal lobe on SWI
Patent major intracranial vessels on MRA
mri.impression: Posterior-predominant cortical atrophy involving bilateral parietal and occipital lobes (right greater than left) with relative sparing of the medial temporal lobes. This pattern is atypical for Alzheimer disease but could represent posterior cortical atrophy. There is also moderate small vessel ischemic disease (Fazekas grade 2) with two chronic lacunar infarcts and rare microbleeds, raising the possibility of a vascular contribution to cognitive symptoms. Hippocampal volumes are age-appropriate, which argues against typical amnestic Alzheimer disease.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Posterior cortical atrophy (PCA) — atypical Alzheimer disease variant

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Moderate — significant white matter disease and lacunar infarcts
Low-moderate — posterior-predominant asymmetric atrophy can be seen

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Lewy body dementia with posterior cortical involvement

recommended_actions: Formal neuropsychological testing with emphasis on visuospatial and visuoperceptual function
Consider amyloid PET to evaluate for underlying Alzheimer pathology
Consider FDG-PET to characterize the metabolic pattern
Clinical correlation with ophthalmological evaluation to exclude primary visual pathology

BMP.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, consistent with known type 2 diabetes mellitus

Thyroid.clinical_significance: Normal thyroid function; hypothyroid dementia excluded

Dementia_workup.clinical_significance: Consistent with known diabetes mellitus, moderately controlled

labs.interpretation: Laboratory evaluation reveals well-controlled type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 6.8%, fasting glucose 112 mg/dL). All other values are within normal limits. Reversible causes of cognitive impairment including hypothyroidism, vitamin B12 deficiency, folate deficiency, neurosyphilis, and HIV have been excluded. Inflammatory markers are normal. The mildly elevated glucose is consistent with the patient's known diabetes and is not an explanation for her visuospatial symptoms.
abnormal_values_summary: HbA1c 6.8% (consistent with known type 2 diabetes mellitus)

Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Within normal range but lost points on visuospatial items; not sensitive to posterior cortical dysfunction
Below cutoff; deficits concentrated in visuospatial/executive domain (cube copy, clock drawing, trail-making)

Neuropsychological_Battery.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate Recall
Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Verbal learning within normal limits — atypical for classic Alzheimer disease

Neuropsychological_Battery.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed Recall
Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Delayed verbal recall preserved, not consistent with hippocampal-type amnesia
Severely impaired visuoconstructional ability; fragmented piecemeal approach, lost spatial configuration
Poor recall of complex figure, though partly attributable to severely impaired initial copy

Neuropsychological_Battery.test: Visual Object and Space Perception Battery (VOSP) - Incomplete Letters
Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Impaired visual perception for degraded stimuli
Severely impaired spatial localization consistent with dorsal stream dysfunction
Impaired — likely reflects visuospatial scanning difficulty rather than pure processing speed deficit
Mildly impaired, confounded by visuospatial scanning demands of the task
Mild depressive symptoms, likely reactive to cognitive decline

output.interpretation: Comprehensive neuropsychological testing reveals a striking dissociation between severely impaired visuospatial and visuoperceptual functions and relatively preserved episodic memory, language, and semantic knowledge. The patient demonstrates severe deficits on all visuoconstructional tasks (Rey Complex Figure copy at 2nd percentile), visual perception (VOSP Incomplete Letters at 3rd percentile), and spatial localization (VOSP Number Location at 1st percentile). In contrast, verbal episodic memory is intact (RAVLT delayed recall at 30th percentile), naming is preserved, and semantic fluency is normal. This cognitive profile is characteristic of posterior cortical atrophy (PCA), a visual variant of Alzheimer disease involving predominant dysfunction of the parietal and occipital association cortices. The pattern is distinctly different from typical amnestic Alzheimer disease and could be confused with a primary ophthalmological or vascular etiology. Mild depressive symptoms are present, likely reactive. Amyloid biomarker testing is strongly recommended to determine the underlying pathology.
abnormal_values_summary: MoCA 23/30 (below cutoff, visuospatial items account for most lost points)
Rey Complex Figure Copy at 2nd percentile - severely impaired
Rey Complex Figure Delayed Recall at 5th percentile
VOSP Number Location at 1st percentile - severely impaired

findings.location: Diffuse cortical uptake with posterior predominance, most intense in parietal, occipital, and posterior cingulate cortex

signal_characteristics.Distribution: Diffuse cortical binding with posterior predominance; highest tracer retention in bilateral parietal and occipital association cortex, precuneus, and posterior cingulate; frontal and temporal uptake also elevated but less prominent

additional_observations: The posterior-predominant amyloid distribution is consistent with but not specific to posterior cortical atrophy variant of Alzheimer disease
No focal asymmetry suggesting alternative pathology
output.impression: POSITIVE amyloid PET scan. Diffuse cortical amyloid deposition with posterior predominance. In the clinical context of progressive visuospatial dysfunction with posterior cortical atrophy on structural MRI, this finding strongly supports a diagnosis of posterior cortical atrophy as a visual variant of Alzheimer disease. The positive amyloid PET confirms underlying Alzheimer pathology as the etiology rather than a non-Alzheimer degenerative process such as corticobasal degeneration or Lewy body disease.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Posterior cortical atrophy — Alzheimer disease variant
Incidental amyloid positivity with separate vascular etiology
differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Low — amyloid prevalence in age-matched population ~15%, and clinical syndrome matches PCA

recommended_actions: Integrate with neuropsychological profile for definitive PCA diagnosis
Discuss diagnosis and prognosis with patient and family
Occupational therapy referral for adaptive strategies for visuospatial deficits

findings.location: Bilateral parieto-occipital cortex and posterior cingulate, right greater than left

signal_characteristics.FDG_PET: Markedly reduced FDG uptake in bilateral parietal and occipital association cortices with relative sparing of the medial temporal lobes and primary visual cortex
signal_characteristics.Pattern: Posterior-predominant hypometabolism involving lateral parietal, superior occipital association cortex, and precuneus/posterior cingulate. Right hemisphere more affected than left. Frontal, primary motor, primary visual, and medial temporal cortex relatively preserved.

additional_observations: Medial temporal lobe metabolism preserved — atypical for classic Alzheimer disease but characteristic of posterior cortical atrophy variant
Primary visual cortex metabolism preserved, suggesting higher-order visual processing dysfunction rather than primary visual cortex pathology
Basal ganglia and thalamic metabolism normal
Cerebellar metabolism preserved and used as reference region
output.impression: FDG-PET demonstrates a posterior-predominant pattern of cortical hypometabolism involving bilateral parieto-occipital association cortex and posterior cingulate/precuneus, right greater than left, with striking preservation of medial temporal lobe metabolism. This pattern is characteristic of posterior cortical atrophy (PCA) and is distinctly different from the temporoparietal hypometabolism seen in typical Alzheimer disease. It is also different from the occipital hypometabolism of Lewy body dementia (which involves primary visual cortex) and the frontal/anterior temporal pattern of frontotemporal dementia.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Low — would expect more asymmetric fronto-parietal pattern

recommended_actions: Correlate with amyloid PET or CSF biomarkers to confirm Alzheimer pathology
Detailed ophthalmological evaluation to exclude compounding visual pathology
Neuropsychological testing with emphasis on visuospatial and visuoperceptual function

output.interpretation: CSF biomarker profile shows equivocal results that complicate interpretation. Amyloid beta-42 is decreased at 420 pg/mL with a reduced Abeta42/40 ratio of 0.054, indicating amyloid positivity (A+). However, phosphorylated tau is borderline at 52 pg/mL (just below the 60 pg/mL cutoff) and total tau is high-normal at 355 pg/mL. This yields an A+T-/borderline N-/borderline profile, which is less definitive than the classic A+T+N+ pattern seen in established Alzheimer disease. Neurofilament light chain is mildly elevated, indicating active neurodegeneration but is a non-specific marker. The biomarker profile is consistent with early Alzheimer pathology but does not exclude other neurodegenerative processes. In the context of the posterior cortical atrophy clinical syndrome, the amyloid positivity supports Alzheimer disease as the underlying pathology, even though the tau markers have not yet reached clearly pathological levels. Prion disease is excluded by negative 14-3-3 and RT-QuIC.

APOE_Genotyping.clinical_significance: Most common genotype. Does not carry the APOE e4 risk allele. Note: posterior cortical atrophy variant of Alzheimer disease is less strongly associated with APOE e4 than typical amnestic AD.

Early_Onset_Panel.clinical_significance: Presenilin-1 mutations are the most common cause of autosomal dominant early-onset AD; none found
No amyloid precursor protein mutations identified

output.interpretation: APOE genotyping reveals the E3/E3 genotype, the most common in the general population, without the e4 risk allele. Notably, the posterior cortical atrophy variant of Alzheimer disease has a weaker association with APOE e4 than typical amnestic AD, so the absence of e4 does not argue against the diagnosis. Given the patient's age of onset at 65, an early-onset Alzheimer panel (PSEN1, PSEN2, APP) was performed and revealed no pathogenic variants. The genetic results are neither supportive nor contradictory of the diagnosis.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha activity over occipital regions, within normal limits

activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response at 10-14 Hz; no photoparoxysmal response

output.impression: Normal EEG for age. The posterior dominant rhythm is 9 Hz and symmetric. No focal slowing, no epileptiform discharges, and no electrographic seizures. This normal EEG does not exclude a neurodegenerative process, as EEG may remain normal in the early stages of posterior cortical atrophy.
output.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording in the awake state only.
recommended_actions: EEG is not the primary diagnostic tool for posterior cortical atrophy; clinical, neuropsychological, and biomarker evaluation are more informative
Consider repeat EEG if seizures are suspected clinically

interactions: No significant interactions with amlodipine, metformin, rosuvastatin, aspirin, or vitamin D3
warnings: May cause bradycardia; monitor heart rate, especially given concurrent amlodipine (which does not typically cause bradycardia but combination should be noted)
GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea, anorexia) common at initiation; monitor in diabetic patient on metformin which can also cause GI symptoms
Use caution with concurrent anticholinergic medications
alternatives: Rivastigmine (patch or oral) - alternative cholinesterase inhibitor

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Posterior cortical atrophy (visual variant of early-stage Alzheimer's disease)

differential.likelihood: Moderate initially, low after biomarker testing
differential.key_distinguishing: Fazekas grade 2 white matter disease and lacunar infarcts are present on MRI, creating a plausible vascular explanation. However, the progressive visuospatial syndrome with preserved memory is atypical for vascular dementia, and amyloid PET positivity confirms underlying Alzheimer pathology.

Posterior-predominant asymmetric atrophy and ideomotor apraxia could suggest CBD. However, the patient lacks rigidity, alien limb phenomenon, cortical sensory loss in a unilateral distribution, or myoclonus. Positive amyloid PET strongly favors Alzheimer disease over CBD.

Visual processing deficits could raise suspicion for LBD, but the patient lacks visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, fluctuating cognition, and REM sleep behavior disorder. FDG-PET spares primary visual cortex (LBD typically shows occipital hypometabolism).

Mild cataracts are present but ophthalmological evaluation confirmed corrected acuity of 20/25, insufficient to explain the severe visuospatial deficits. The higher-order nature of the deficits (simultanagnosia, optic ataxia, spatial disorientation) points to cortical rather than ocular pathology.

Mild reactive depressive symptoms (GDS 6/15) are present but insufficient to explain the severe, domain-specific visuospatial deficits on neuropsychological testing. The progressive nature over 18 months and structural/biomarker findings argue strongly against pseudodementia.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history from patient and informant with specific attention to visuospatial symptoms, reading difficulty, navigational impairment, and preserved memory function
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive visuospatial dysfunction over 18 months with relatively preserved episodic memory — an atypical pattern for Alzheimer disease

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination including detailed cortical sensory testing, assessment for Balint syndrome components, Gerstmann syndrome elements, and ideomotor apraxia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: MMSE 26/30 with visuospatial deficits; impaired graphesthesia, stereognosis, simultanagnosia, optic ataxia, finger agnosia, right-left confusion, and ideomotor apraxia

optimal_actions.action: Order dementia screening labs: CBC, BMP, TSH, vitamin B12, folate, RPR, HIV, HbA1c
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal except for mildly elevated glucose and HbA1c consistent with known diabetes; reversible causes excluded

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with volumetric protocol
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Posterior-predominant cortical atrophy with preserved hippocampal volumes; white matter disease and lacunar infarcts creating diagnostic uncertainty

optimal_actions.action: Request comprehensive neuropsychological testing with emphasis on visuospatial and visuoperceptual functions (VOSP battery)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Severe visuospatial and visuoperceptual deficits with preserved episodic memory and language — characteristic PCA neuropsychological profile

optimal_actions.action: Order amyloid PET (florbetapir or florbetaben) to determine if Alzheimer pathology underlies the posterior cortical atrophy syndrome
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive amyloid PET confirming Alzheimer disease as the underlying pathology

optimal_actions.action: Consider FDG-PET to characterize the metabolic pattern and differentiate from other etiologies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Posterior-predominant parieto-occipital hypometabolism with preserved medial temporal metabolism — classic PCA pattern

optimal_actions.action: Ensure ophthalmological evaluation has been performed to exclude primary visual pathology contributing to symptoms
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild cataracts insufficient to explain symptoms; corrected visual acuity 20/25

optimal_actions.action: Initiate cholinesterase inhibitor therapy (donepezil 5 mg daily) — evidence is extrapolated from typical AD trials
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant drug interactions with current medications

optimal_actions.action: Provide diagnosis disclosure and counseling emphasizing the atypical nature of this Alzheimer variant, prognosis, safety planning (especially driving cessation), adaptive strategies for visuospatial deficits, and advance directives
Refer to occupational therapy for adaptive strategies specific to visuospatial deficits (home safety modifications, reading aids, environmental simplification)
Strongly recommend driving cessation given visuospatial impairment and refer for formal driving evaluation if patient resists

critical_actions: Recognize that visuospatial-predominant cognitive decline with preserved memory suggests posterior cortical atrophy rather than typical Alzheimer disease
Do not dismiss symptoms as ophthalmological when visual acuity is relatively preserved and deficits are higher-order cortical
Order amyloid biomarker testing (PET or CSF) to confirm Alzheimer pathology as the underlying cause of PCA
Perform detailed cortical sensory examination including assessment for Balint and Gerstmann syndrome features
Do not be misled by the white matter disease and lacunar infarcts on MRI into diagnosing vascular dementia as the sole etiology
Exclude reversible causes of cognitive decline with laboratory testing
Address driving safety — this patient is at high risk for accidents due to visuospatial impairment
Request neuropsychological testing that includes visuospatial-specific measures (e.g., VOSP) rather than relying solely on MMSE which is insensitive to PCA
contraindicated_actions: Attributing all symptoms to cataracts or refractive error without considering cortical pathology
Diagnosing vascular dementia based on white matter disease alone without considering the atypical clinical phenotype
Prescribing anticholinergic medications (e.g., diphenhydramine, oxybutynin) which worsen cognitive function
Prescribing benzodiazepines without careful consideration of their effects on cognition and fall risk in a patient with visuospatial impairment
Dismissing the symptoms as normal aging or anxiety because the MMSE is near-normal (26/30)
Starting memantine monotherapy in early/mild disease
Ordering brain biopsy when non-invasive biomarker confirmation is available
key_reasoning_points: Posterior cortical atrophy (PCA) is a visual variant of Alzheimer disease characterized by progressive visuospatial, visuoperceptual, and literacy deficits with relatively preserved episodic memory early in the course
PCA typically presents between ages 50-65, which is younger than typical Alzheimer disease, and has a weaker association with APOE e4

The near-normal MMSE (26/30) is a diagnostic trap — MMSE is heavily weighted toward orientation and memory and is insensitive to posterior cortical dysfunction; MoCA is somewhat better but still underestimates the severity

Key examination features include elements of Balint syndrome (simultanagnosia, optic ataxia, ocular apraxia) and Gerstmann syndrome (finger agnosia, right-left confusion, agraphia, acalculia), which localize to the parieto-occipital cortex
The preserved hippocampal volumes on MRI argue against typical amnestic AD but are consistent with PCA, where atrophy is posterior-predominant

The concurrent Fazekas grade 2 white matter disease and lacunar infarcts are a red herring that could lead to a misdiagnosis of vascular dementia — the clinical phenotype does not match a vascular pattern

Amyloid PET or CSF biomarkers are essential to confirm that Alzheimer pathology underlies the clinical syndrome, as PCA can rarely be caused by non-Alzheimer pathologies (Lewy body disease, corticobasal degeneration, prion disease)

The equivocal CSF tau markers (borderline phospho-tau, high-normal total tau) add to the diagnostic difficulty — the amyloid markers are more clearly positive and are the most informative for diagnosis

key_educational_points: Posterior cortical atrophy is an under-recognized variant of Alzheimer disease that presents with visual/spatial rather than memory symptoms
MMSE is insensitive to posterior cortical dysfunction — use visuospatial-specific tests like VOSP
Preserved hippocampal volumes do not rule out Alzheimer disease — atypical variants spare the medial temporal lobes early
Concurrent vascular disease on MRI can mislead the diagnostician when the clinical phenotype does not fit a vascular pattern
Amyloid biomarkers are crucial for confirming the Alzheimer pathological basis of PCA
